note: PLAN-3 R3. Anchors: Chaunu & García-Baquero (Carrera); the SlaveVoyages Trans-Atlantic Database (the best-counted trade of the era); Brazilian frota records; English/French customs & port books; Pope & Cell (Newfoundland).
anchors: P. & H. Chaunu, Séville et l'Atlantique; A. García-Baquero (Cádiz)
J. H. Parry; R. Davis, The Rise of the English Shipping Industry

systems.name: The Carrera de Indias
systems.basis: Chaunu, Séville et l'Atlantique; García-Baquero for the Cádiz century; both directions loaded (goods out, silver home). Seville and Cádiz lanes carried at constant shares — the 1717 transfer is smeared, noted.
systems.notes: Convoyed flotas → register ships → comercio libre (1778 boom) → wars and independence.

systems.name: The Brazil fleets (sugar → gold → the court)
systems.basis: Portuguese frota records and Bahia/Rio port studies; sugar boom c.1600, gold era 1700s–60s, the court at Rio and open ports after 1808.

systems.name: The Middle Passage
systems.basis: SlaveVoyages — the Trans-Atlantic Slave Trade Database: ~36,000 documented voyages; the era's best-counted trade, because people were cargo in its ledgers. Counted here as embarkation legs.

framing.label: coerced human movement
framing.description: Human beings trafficked against their will across the Atlantic — the largest forced migration in history. Mortality on the passage was immense.
framing.rule: No value framing, no profit framing, never a reward. Sim-side representation waits for S2's generalization of the Middle-Passage invariant; until then these flows exist as historical record only.

systems.name: The Guinea outward legs
systems.basis: The same database, outward-leg side: European and Brazilian barter cargoes to the African coast. The African merchant polities (Efik houses, Dahomey, the Gold Coast states) set the terms of this exchange — see powers.json.

systems.name: Iberian & Gascon wine and salt, northbound
systems.basis: English and Dutch port books; the Bordeaux wine fleets and the Lisbon/Cádiz trades to northwest Europe (the Baltic salt run is carried in the Baltic basin).

systems.name: The Newfoundland fishery and the sack trade
systems.basis: Cell & Pope; English West Country, French, and Basque fleets to the Banks; the 'sack ships' carrying cod direct to Iberia and the Mediterranean.

systems.name: The West India sugar trades
systems.basis: English and French customs: the sugar fleets from Jamaica, Barbados, and Saint-Domingue that remade Atlantic Europe.
systems.notes: Cap-Français lanes effectively end 1791 — the constant shares smear this; the rankings carry the event.

systems.name: The Chesapeake tobacco trade
systems.basis: English customs and the Glasgow tobacco lords' records.

systems.name: The New England–Caribbean provisioning triangle
systems.basis: Colonial naval office lists: cod, lumber, and horses south; rum, sugar, and molasses north — the trade that built the North American ports.

systems.name: The Caribbean inter-island contraband trade
systems.basis: ASSERTED (R3 decision) — silent BY EVASION: unrecorded because recording was the danger. Central guesses from the free-port scholarship (St Eustatius's 3,000+ annual ship calls at its 1770s peak, Curaçao, Monte Cristi, the asiento's shadow); bounds ±60%.
systems.notes: The register's own entry: this trade is in the record chiefly as seizures and complaints.

systems.name: The Davis Strait & Greenland whale fishery
systems.basis: British bounty records and Dutch Groenlandvaart lists — the Arctic fishery's 18th-c westward shift.

systems.name: The Dutch Guianas: coffee and sugar
systems.basis: Suriname's plantation exports, Dutch from 1667. The commodity anchor is CLEAN and counted - the coffee peak of 7,615 tonnes across 1772-76 - but what is counted is coffee, not sailings, so the voyage ranges here are proxied from produce volumes against typical Dutch West-Indiaman capacities and are classed accordingly. Genuinely distinct from caribbean-sugar: a different colony, a different metropole, and coffee rather than sugar as the lead commodity.
systems.notes: Roughly 250,000 to 275,000 enslaved people were carried to the Dutch Guianas to work these plantations; the colony's mortality was such that it required continuous new arrivals to sustain a smaller population. That movement is NOT given a lane or a cargo here, because it is already carried under middle-passage and a second series would double-count the same people. It is recorded so that this system's coffee and sugar are never read as though they arrived on their own. The 1780s and 1800s dips are the Fourth Anglo-Dutch War and the British occupations. Runs to 1850: Suriname stayed Dutch and kept exporting after the coffee peak passed.

systems.name: The Bay settlement: logwood and mahogany
systems.basis: The Bay of Honduras cutting settlement, English-speaking from the 1660s - deliberately NOT dated to the contested 1638 - shipping dyewood and later mahogany to Britain, with a Jamaica re-export leg. Reconstructed from tonnage descriptions against timber-carrier capacities: roughly 200 tonnes a year in the 1570s Campeche trade rising to a figure of about 13,000 tonnes in a PEAK year, which is a maximum and not a mean and wants Bolland behind it before it carries more weight. Mahogany displaces logwood from the 1770s. No overlap with caribbean-sugar or caribbean-smuggling: a distinct commodity on its own lanes.
systems.notes: The wood was cut by enslaved people. Cutting gangs worked far up the rivers from the settlement, and the labour was neither free nor incidental to the trade - it was the trade. No cargo or lane represents that movement here, for the same reason as the Guianas: those arrivals belong to middle-passage and would double-count. Recorded so the timber is not read as though it cut itself. The 1740s dip is the Spanish attacks on the settlement. Runs past 1815: the mahogany trade grew through the nineteenth century.
